skill.name: multi-agent-patterns
skill.description: Guidance for designing orchestrator, peer-to-peer, and hierarchical multi-agent systems.
skill.summary: Plan reliable multi-agent architectures with clear coordination and failure controls.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 34 findings, but review found the external command, weak cryptography, HTTP client, and system reconnaissance findings are false positives from Markdown fences, examples, and prose. The only confirmed risk factor is user-visible external documentation links; no executable command, cryptographic operation, automatic network request, data exfiltration, or prompt injection evidence was found.

low_findings.title: External Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The skill includes a source URL and reference links to external documentation and research. These links are visible Markdown references, not automatic network requests, so the risk is limited to normal external-link trust.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are directly present at the cited lines. They are static references and no code path performs an automatic request.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Fences Misread as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The static Ruby or shell backtick detections correspond to Markdown code fences, diagrams, and illustrative Python snippets. No shell command execution or user-controlled command construction is present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown fences and non-executed examples. There is no Ruby code, shell backtick expression, subprocess call, or command string.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Prose Misread as Crypto or Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance detections land on frontmatter, headings, and conceptual prose about agent systems. No cryptographic algorithm, credential handling, host inspection, or environment reconnaissance appears at the cited lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of every cited line shows architecture discussion, not executable crypto or reconnaissance behavior. The signals are terminology matches without operational code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: HTTP Client Library Detection
low_findings.description: The HTTP client library detection points to an example function signature for routing a customer request. No import, HTTP client call, URL fetch, or network transmission is present at that line.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 209 starts an illustrative Python function and contains no HTTP client usage. Nearby lines only show local branching to conceptual agents.

content.value_statement: Complex AI work often fails when one agent carries too much context and coordination load. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code users choose practical multi-agent patterns with explicit handoffs and failure controls.
actual_capabilities: Explains supervisor, peer-to-peer, and hierarchical agent patterns.
Compares when each architecture is appropriate for complex tasks.
Describes context isolation as the main benefit of sub-agents.
Identifies common failure modes such as bottlenecks, divergence, and error propagation.
Provides example handoff and direct-forwarding patterns for agent coordination.
Lists framework considerations for LangGraph, AutoGen, and CrewAI.
limitations: It provides architecture guidance, not a runnable multi-agent framework.
It does not include implementation files beyond the Markdown skill document.
It depends on the user to adapt examples to their chosen agent platform.
It does not benchmark or validate a deployed multi-agent system.

use_cases.title: Plan a research agent team
use_cases.description: Design a supervisor-led workflow that separates retrieval, analysis, verification, and writing.

use_cases.title: Select an architecture pattern
use_cases.description: Compare orchestrator, swarm, and hierarchical designs before building a complex agent workflow.

use_cases.title: Review coordination risks
use_cases.description: Audit an existing multi-agent design for bottlenecks, weak handoffs, and missing convergence checks.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the multi-agent-patterns skill to recommend one architecture for this task: [describe task]. Explain why it fits.

prompt_templates.title: Define Agent Roles
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the multi-agent-patterns skill to break this workflow into agents, responsibilities, inputs, and outputs: [workflow].

prompt_templates.title: Design Handoffs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the multi-agent-patterns skill to design explicit handoff rules, shared state, and validation checks for: [agent system].

prompt_templates.title: Stress Test Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the multi-agent-patterns skill to critique this architecture for context overload, divergence, error propagation, and token cost: [architecture].

output_examples.input: Design a multi-agent workflow for a market research report.
output: Agents: researcher, analyst, fact-checker, and writer.
Key control: allow direct forwarding for final verified sections.
Main risk: supervisor context overload from full source summaries.

output_examples.input: Review a peer-to-peer support agent system.
output: Strength: flexible routing between billing, technical, and sales agents.
Risk: divergence if agents transfer control without shared state.
Mitigation: require handoff summaries, ownership markers, and time-to-live limits.

best_practices: Use multi-agent designs only when the task benefits from context isolation or parallel work.
Define handoff protocols, output schemas, and validation checks before implementation.
Test bottlenecks, divergence, error propagation, and token cost under realistic workloads.
anti_patterns: Do not split work into agents only to imitate an organization chart.
Do not let every agent see every prior message when isolation is the goal.
Do not rely on simple majority voting for complex reasoning without critique or weighting.

faq.question: Does this skill run a multi-agent system?
faq.answer: No. It is a planning and architecture guide for designing multi-agent systems.

faq.question: Which architecture patterns does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers supervisor, peer-to-peer, swarm, and hierarchical multi-agent patterns.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides design guidance that can be applied while building with Claude Code, Codex, or Claude.

faq.question: Does it include framework-specific code?
faq.answer: No. It mentions LangGraph, AutoGen, and CrewAI, but the content is mostly framework-neutral.

faq.question: What is the main design principle?
faq.answer: The main principle is context isolation. Sub-agents should reduce context load and coordination complexity.

faq.question: When should I avoid multi-agent designs?
faq.answer: Avoid them for simple tasks where added coordination, latency, and token cost outweigh the benefit.
